content:
9 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The agents improvise. The tower is not allowed to.Every Claude Code session on my machine reports to a control tower that has made exactly zero model calls.
The tower is Fleet Deck, a side project I built for a problem I created myself. I run several Claude Code sessions in parallel, spread across terminals, tmux windows, and git worktrees, and I kept losing track of which agent was working, which one had been waiting twenty minutes for a permission click, and which two were about to edit the same file. Now every session appears on one local board with a callsign, a live status column, a mailbox, and a conflict radar. The board can spawn new agents into tmux windows and git worktrees, open their live terminals in the browser, revive them after a reboot, and clean up after them when they are done.
The numbers, because a claim without numbers is a vibe: one Node daemon at about 7.5k lines, a React board at 6.7k, 303 contract tests. The whole thing ships as a single 324 KB file with exactly one runtime dependency, the ws WebSocket library. SQLite comes from node:sqlite, built into Node since 22.5 and running without a flag since 22.13. There is nothing to npm install on the machine that runs it.
And the line I care most about: the core makes zero model calls. Routing, conflict detection, question relay, and every verdict in the system are deterministic code and SQL.
Here’s the thing. When your payload is a fleet of nondeterministic agents, the machinery around them has to be the most boring code you own. This first post is about that machinery, because the boring parts are where the engineering went. Part 2 covers the bugs that made it past everything anyway. Part 3 covers what the agents that helped build it got right and wrong along the way.
The election is a port bind
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The port bind is the election. Losing is free.There is no supervisor. No systemd unit, no launcher, no process manager watching the process manager. When a session starts, its SessionStart hook tries to launch the daemon. If a daemon is already up, the new one loses the EADDRINUSE race on port 4711 and exits with code 3. The port bind is the election.
This looks like laziness and it is the opposite. Every coordination scheme needs an arbiter, and the usual arbiters bring their own failure modes. Lock files need crash cleanup. PID files lie after a reboot. A supervisor is one more daemon that can die, and then you have two problems. The kernel already refuses to hand the same port to two processes, atomically, with nothing to clean up on any failure path. The oldest primitive on the box turned out to be the right one.
One honest asterisk: the state directory keeps a pid guard of its own, so a daemon on a different port cannot adopt a home another daemon already owns. That guard protects the database, not the election. The election for the port is still the bind.
Losing is free, too. Exit 3, no error surfaced into the session, no retry storm. Whoever wins, wins for everyone.
Sessions believe they are almost done
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sessions are never asked what they are doing. Sessions, like people, believe they are almost done.The column a session occupies on the board (queued, working, verifying, needs-you, idle, offline) is never self-reported. It is derived from hook telemetry, every time. SessionStart lands you in queued. A prompt from the human moves you to working. Tool activity keeps you there, unless the Bash command matches a test-runner pattern, which flips the card to verifying. A permission dialog or an open question moves you to needs-you. Stop means idle. SessionEnd means offline.
Sessions are never asked what they are doing. Sessions, like people, believe they are almost done.
Ask an agent mid-task how it is going and you will get optimism. The telemetry cannot be charmed. Derived state also self-heals: a card presumed dead after three hours of silence gets revived by any late hook, which is what keeps the board honest across laptop suspends. And the same property makes revive nearly free. claude --resume keeps the same session id, so a revived agent's first hook re-attaches it to its old card. Same callsign, full history, no new bookkeeping.
A dashboard that can wedge your work is a liability
Every hook is fail-open. Daemon down, daemon slow, daemon on fire: hooks time out silently and your session runs exactly as it would without the plugin installed. An internal error inside a hook handler still returns 200 {} to the CLI. The rule has no exceptions.
The strictest test of that rule is the needs-you relay. When a session raises a permission request, the daemon creates a question card and parks the HTTP response, holding it open. The terminal sits there, waiting. Answer on the board and the parked response resolves with your decision; the terminal prints ⎿ Allowed by PermissionRequest hook and carries on. If nobody answers within 50 seconds, the hold resolves with {} and normal terminal flow resumes, exactly as if Fleet Deck were not there.
You can always ignore the board. That sentence was a design requirement, and half the architecture falls out of it. Fleet Deck is a tower, not a runway. It watches, advises, and relays. The planes fly themselves, and a tower that could ground your fleet by crashing is a tower nobody should install.
No shell, no PTY, no cookie
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Three refusals shaped everything the daemon touches.Three refusals shaped everything the daemon does with the outside world.
No shell. Every tmux command is an argv array through execFile. Never a shell string containing user text, anywhere. A prompt containing ; rm -rf ~ arrives in the child as literal bytes in an argument, because no shell exists to interpret it. The discipline extends to typing: there are exactly three sanctioned ways anything gets typed into an agent's pane. One Enter keypress, 8 seconds after spawn, to clear the trust dialog. Mail delivery through bracketed paste, so a multi-line message cannot submit itself early. And the human's own keystrokes, relayed verbatim as hex through the terminal bridge. Nothing else ever touches a fleet pane's stdin.
The doctrine pays for itself in strange places. Flipping a live agent into remote-control mode has no API, so the daemon types /rc <callsign> into the pane through the sanctioned mail machinery, then harvests the URL from the pane's scrollback with capture-pane, because that URL is written to no file and no transcript. Scrollback is the only programmatic source. And the guard: refused unless the session is queued or idle, because typing a slash command into a session with a permission dialog open would answer the dialog instead.
Spawning itself is arranged so nothing ever guesses. The spawn endpoint pre-issues the session UUID before tmux even runs, which is how a card can exist on the board while the pane is still booting. Windows get scoped names, and a kill re-locates the window by exact name at kill time and kills by window id, so a renamed window can never be mis-killed through a stale index. Panes run with remain-on-exit on, because a dead pane is the deterministic crash signal and the human may want the scrollback. It also means tmux keeps reporting the original command after death, so you must read pane_dead too. Trust nothing that can go stale.
No PTY. The live terminal in the browser does not use node-pty. Each viewer gets a tmux control-mode client, the same protocol iTerm2 uses for its tmux integration, speaking over plain pipes. Output arrives as %output notifications, keystrokes go back as hex-encoded send-keys, and every viewer gets its own virtual size. The win is real: no native module, which is how the bundle stays one 324 KB file. The cost is also real: you implement the protocol yourself, and the protocol has teeth. Part 2 has the scars.
No cookie. Bind the board to the LAN (FLEETDECK_BIND=0.0.0.0) and a bearer token becomes mandatory; the daemon refuses to start without one. An unauthenticated API that can spawn --dangerously-skip-permissions agents and type into their terminals is remote code execution with a nice font. And the token is deliberately a header, never a cookie. A cookie rides along on cross-site requests, and the browser defenses against that are a patchwork: Chrome now defaults cookies to SameSite=Lax and puts local network requests behind a permission, Safari still attaches a default cookie to a cross-site form POST, and one SameSite=None mistake reopens the hole everywhere. A bearer header removes the whole class. No page you visit can make your browser attach it. Loopback stays exempt from the token, because hooks are local and must keep working with zero configuration; even there, data routes check a browser's Origin header, so a random page cannot script the board. Remember that exemption. It stars in Part 3.
LAN discovery, for completeness, is a hand-rolled mDNS responder with zero dependencies that degrades to a silent no-op when a real Avahi already holds port 5353 or the network eats multicast. Discovery is a convenience, never a dependency. The README says plainly that cross-device reception is unproven: verified same-host with dig, and Windows on the same box did not resolve it. I would rather document that sentence than delete it.
What the determinism buys
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The radar warns. It never blocks.The conflict radar is my favorite example of how far plain SQL goes. Every file-touching event lands in a ledger keyed by repo and relative path. Another session touched the same path within 30 minutes? Conflict. Same worktree gets a warning, because an overwrite is imminent. Different worktrees of the same repo get an info, because that is a merge conflict introducing itself early. A touch counts even if its session already ended; the uncommitted edits it left behind are exactly what you are about to clobber.
Delivery is matched to urgency, and none of it needed a model. The session that just touched the file gets a whisper injected straight into its context, right after the tool call. The rival sessions get fleet mail. You get a ticker line on the board. The radar warns. It never blocks.
Mail itself has four delivery paths, and the board is honest about which one you are on. Queued mail rides into the next prompt as context. At a turn boundary, the daemon can hand it to the session directly. An idle session has a watcher that long-polls the daemon and wakes it within seconds. A freshly spawned session that has never taken a turn gets the mail typed into its pane through the bracketed-paste path above. The board reports watcher, pane, turn-boundary, or offline-queued instead of promising instant, because promising instant is a lie you eventually get caught in.
Even Clear is a rule, not a mood: archive what is offline, keep a conflict only if every participant is still alive, list orphaned worktrees and delete none of them. And when something looks wrong, I do not read tea leaves. I ask the daemon what it believes:
sqlite3 ~/.fleetdeck/fleetd.db \
'SELECT callsign, col, note, events FROM sessions ORDER BY last_seen DESC'Deterministic state means that query is the truth, reproducible, and arguable in plain SQL. That is what makes the tower debuggable, and debuggable is what makes it trustworthy. It matters, because things still went wrong. Part 2 is the story of the bugs that made it past a green suite: a ghost daemon my agents reported to for a full day, a glyph that arrived in two pieces, and a git verdict that cried wolf.
The agents improvise. The tower is not allowed to.
